articles.spearman: The [icon=spearman 18]Spearman is an Ancient Era melee unit, the backbone of every early army. Its long spear forms an impenetrable wall against charging horsemen. Combat Statistics Strength 7 Hit Points 30 Movement 2 Production 60 Abilities - Phalanx -- +100% combat strength against [icon=cavalry 18]Cavalry and Chariots. - Zone of Control -- Enemy units spend all remaining movement when leaving an adjacent tile. Weaknesses Spearmen struggle against dedicated anti-infantry. Swordsmen backed by Iron cut through spear formations, and ranged fire from [icon=archer 18]Archers can whittle down a phalanx before it reaches melee range. Siege equipment on high ground is also devastating. Upgrade Path Spearman -> Pikeman -> Musketman Historical Notes The spear is humanity's oldest purpose-built weapon. Sumerian phalanxes at Lagash (c. 2450 BCE) drilled tight formations of spear-bearers locked shield to shield. Greek city-states refined this into the hoplite phalanx -- eight ranks deep, each man overlapping his neighbour's aspis -- a wall of bronze that shattered Persian cavalry at Marathon in 490 BCE. Macedonian king Philip II lengthened the shaft into the sarissa, a pike up to six metres long. His son Alexander carried the phalanx to the edges of the known world, defeating every opponent from Granicus to Gaugamela. The Romans replaced the phalanx with the flexible maniple system, but the spearman never truly vanished -- Swiss Reislaeufer pikemen were still breaking French chivalry in the 15th century CE.
articles.warrior: [icon=warrior 18] Warriors are the most basic military unit -- disciplined foot soldiers armed with clubs, axes, or short swords. Cheap and fast to produce, they provide cities with essential early defence and can explore nearby terrain in relative safety. Combat Statistics Strength 5 Hit Points 30 Movement 2 Production 40 Upgrade Path Warrior -> Swordsman -> Musketman Historical Notes The warrior predates civilisation itself. Evidence of organised violence -- skeletons with healed and unhealed weapon injuries buried together -- appears in hunter-gatherer cemeteries as early as 10,000 BCE. What changed with the rise of cities was not the fact of warfare but its scale and organisation. Sumerian city-states fielded the first true armies around 2500 BCE: professional soldiers drawing palace rations, equipped with standardised copper-tipped spears and heavy leather cloaks. The Stele of the Vultures, commemorating Lagash's victory over Umma, shows a phalanx of helmeted warriors advancing in lockstep behind a wall of overlapping shields -- a formation that would define infantry combat for four thousand years. The warrior's essential nature changed little across this span. Whether Aztec cuachic veterans or medieval English billmen, the close-combat infantryman remained the unit that took and held ground, and no cavalry or archery could substitute for men willing to stand and fight at arm's reach.
articles.archer: [icon=archer 18] The Archer is the first ranged unit of the Ancient Era -- a disciplined bowman who strikes enemies from two tiles away without entering melee range. Archers excel at softening enemy formations before an assault and at defending cities from elevated positions. Combat Statistics Strength (melee) 4 Strength (ranged) 5 Hit Points 25 Range 2 Movement 2 Production 60 Abilities - Ranged Attack -- Can attack any tile within 2 hexes without moving into it. - Vulnerable in Melee -- -50% combat strength when attacked in close combat. Upgrade Path Archer -> Crossbowman -> Field Cannon Historical Notes The composite bow -- made from laminated wood, bone, and sinew -- was the high-technology weapon of the ancient world. Developed on the Eurasian steppe around 2000 BCE, it stored three times the energy of a simple wooden stave and was compact enough to fire from horseback. Hittite and Egyptian armies that mastered it gained a decisive battlefield edge. At Kadesh (1274 BCE) Egyptian archers rode paired in chariots: one man drove, one fired. Ramesses II's account describes chariot squadrons making repeated passes at Hittite infantry flanks, pouring arrows into formations that could not effectively strike back. The English longbow is perhaps the most studied ranged weapon in history. At Crecy (1346) and Agincourt (1415), English archers loosed twelve arrows per minute against French knights at ranges exceeding 200 metres. Longbowmen's skeletons show deformed spines and asymmetrically enlarged left arms from decades of training begun in childhood.
articles.scout: [icon=scout 18] The Scout is a lightly armed, fast-moving reconnaissance unit trained to navigate difficult terrain and reveal hidden lands. Scouts explore farther and faster than any other unit, uncovering natural wonders, city-state locations, and rival civilisations before they find you. Combat Statistics Strength 2 Hit Points 20 Movement 3 Production 25 Abilities - Woodsman -- Ignores movement penalties in forests and jungles. - Keen Eyes -- Sight radius extended by 1 tile. - Evasion -- Cannot be targeted when a stronger friendly unit is adjacent. Historical Notes Every military culture developed specialist scouts chosen for endurance, woodcraft, and the discipline to observe without being seen. Roman exploratores rode ahead of the legions, mapping roads, locating water, and counting enemy campfires. Native American scouts serving the US Army in the 1870s could track a single horse across bare rock and estimate how many days old the trail was. The consequences of poor reconnaissance were catastrophic. Crassus led three Roman legions into the Syrian desert at Carrhae in 53 BCE without adequate scouting, blundering into a Parthian force ideally suited to the open terrain. His army was destroyed -- 20,000 dead, 10,000 captured -- in one of Rome's worst ever defeats.
articles.settler: [icon=settler 18] The Settler carries a group of colonists equipped to establish a new city. Founding a settlement costs the origin city 1 Population but opens an entirely new site for growth, buildings, and resource exploitation. Settlers cannot attack or defend. Statistics Strength 0 (civilian, cannot fight) Movement 2 Production 80 Notes - Settlers cannot enter territory owned by another civilisation. - Escort Settlers through dangerous terrain -- they are defenceless. - Scout a location before settling; city placement determines tile yields. Historical Notes The decision to establish a new settlement was among the most consequential a community could make. Successful colonisation required a founding group large enough to be self-sustaining, knowledge of the destination's resources, and either peaceful relations with existing inhabitants or the military strength to displace them. Greek apoikia (colonial settlements) were carefully planned expeditions: an oracle at Delphi consulted, a leader (oikist) chosen, and a sacred flame carried from the mother city to light the new hearth. Between 750 and 550 BCE the Greeks established over 400 such settlements across the Mediterranean and Black Sea -- spreading language and culture from Spain to the Crimea. The Roman colonia served a dual purpose: absorbing surplus population and projecting military power. Veterans rewarded with land grants in conquered territory became farmer-soldiers holding terrain the legions had won. Colchester, Gloucester, Lincoln, and York were all Roman coloniae; their street plans shaped those cities for two thousand years.
articles.builder: [icon=builder 18] The Builder is a civilian unit with a limited number of build charges used to construct terrain improvements -- Farms, Mines, Lumber Mills -- on tiles worked by nearby cities. Each improvement permanently boosts yields. Builders are consumed when all charges are spent. Statistics Strength 0 (civilian, cannot fight) Movement 2 Build Charges 3 Production 50 Common Improvements - Farm -- +1 Food on grassland and plains tiles. - Mine -- +1 Production on hills and resource tiles. - Lumber Mill -- +1 Production on forest tiles. Historical Notes The transformation of raw landscape into productive farmland is the defining act of settled civilisation. Irrigation agriculture in Mesopotamia, begun around 6000 BCE, required coordinated community labour: surveying, digging canals, building levees, and managing water allocation across dozens of fields simultaneously. Egyptian officials tracked annual Nile flood levels from at least 3000 BCE, using them to predict crop yields months in advance. Fields were re-surveyed every year after the flood receded because boundary markers washed away -- a practical need that drove early advances in geometry. The word 'geometry' itself means earth measurement. Roman agricultural engineers divided conquered land into a grid of 200-acre squares (centuriae), reorganising entire landscapes for efficient farming. The grid of the Po Valley in northern Italy still follows Roman centuriation lines visible in satellite photographs two thousand years later.
articles.granary: [icon=food 18] The Granary is one of humanity's earliest infrastructure achievements -- a communal storehouse that turns seasonal surplus into year-round security. Cities with a Granary grow faster and can support larger populations without hunger. City Yields Food +2 Housing +1 Production 60 Requirements Unlocked at Pottery. Built in the City Center. Historical Notes The first purpose-built granaries appear in the Levantine Pre-Pottery Neolithic, around 9500 BCE -- centuries before writing or the wheel. At Dhra' in modern Jordan, oval mud-brick pits were lined with woven grass to wick moisture away from stored grain. By 3500 BCE the Sumerians had elevated storage to an administrative science. Temple precincts at Ur and Uruk maintained vast warehouse complexes staffed by scribes who tallied every shekel-weight of barley entering or leaving on clay tablets -- among the oldest written records ever found. Roman horrea (public granaries) were architectural marvels: floors raised on brick pillars to admit circulating air, narrow north-facing windows to minimise heat, and thick south walls against summer sun. Emperor Augustus reorganised the entire grain supply into a professional state administration -- the cura annonae -- recognising that feeding the capital was as much a military problem as a logistical one.
articles.smithy: [icon=production 18] The Smithy is the industrial heart of an ancient city -- a forge where raw ore is smelted into tools, weapons, and building hardware. Cities with a Smithy complete construction projects significantly faster. City Yields Production +2 Production (cost) 80 Requirements Unlocked at Bronze Working. Historical Notes Copper smelting first appears around 5000 BCE in the Balkans and Anatolia, where craftsmen discovered that malachite -- a vivid green ore -- could be reduced to workable metal in a sufficiently hot charcoal fire. Within a millennium, smiths learned that adding tin produced bronze: harder, sharper, and far more durable than copper alone. Bronze smithies were among the most closely guarded facilities of the ancient world. Mycenaean Linear B tablets record specialist smiths (ka-ke-u) receiving rations directly from the palace -- they were state assets, not free tradesmen. The island of Cyprus, whose very name gives us the word 'copper,' became so synonymous with metal production that controlling it was a strategic priority for every Mediterranean power. Iron smelting spread from Anatolia around 1200 BCE. Smiths who learned to carburise and quench wrought iron produced steel superior to any bronze. Because iron ore is vastly more common, the iron smithy democratised metalwork and flooded armies with affordable weaponry, ending the Bronze Age monopoly of palace workshops.
articles.market: [icon=gold 18] The Market is a hub of commercial activity where merchants, artisans, and travellers exchange goods, currency, and information. Cities with a Market generate substantially more gold each turn and may establish an additional Trade Route. City Yields Gold +3 Trade Routes +1 Production 100 Requirements Unlocked at Currency. Requires the Commercial Hub district. Historical Notes The earliest markets were recurring festivals -- neutral ground where groups with otherwise hostile relations could exchange livestock, flint, and obsidian under the protection of custom. The word 'bazaar' comes from the Persian bazar, derived from an Old Iranian root meaning 'the place of prices.' Mesopotamian markets operated under sophisticated legal frameworks. The Code of Hammurabi (c. 1754 BCE) devoted dozens of clauses to commercial contracts: interest rates on grain loans, liability for lost merchandise, and penalties for merchants who falsified weights. Temple complexes served as both banks and commodity exchanges, issuing standardised silver ingots stamped with the temple seal as early currency. The Roman forum began as a combined market and civic space. By the Imperial period specialised markets had proliferated -- the Forum Boarium for cattle, the Forum Piscarium for fish. Trajan's Market (completed c. 113 CE) provided six levels of covered retail space housing over 150 individual shops: the world's first purpose-built shopping complex.
articles.temple: [icon=culture 18] The Temple is a sacred space dedicated to the divine -- the spiritual centre of the city where citizens gather for rites, festivals, and communal identity. Cities with a Temple generate Faith each turn, drawing priests and scholars who enrich civic and cultural life. City Yields Faith +2 Culture +1 Production 90 Requirements Unlocked at Astrology. Requires the Holy Site district. Historical Notes The oldest known temple is Gobekli Tepe in southern Turkey, built around 9600 BCE -- before pottery, before agriculture, perhaps before permanent settlement. Its carved limestone pillars weighing up to 10 tonnes required organised communal labour on a scale that challenges earlier assumptions about pre-agricultural society. Mesopotamian ziggurats were not temples in the modern sense but artificial mountains -- raised platforms that brought the priest-king closer to heaven. The ziggurat of Ur, built by Ur-Nammu around 2100 BCE, soared 30 metres above the flat alluvial plain and was visible for miles, an assertion of divine and civic power simultaneously. The Parthenon (432 BCE) took the temple in a different direction: not a house of interior worship but an exterior monument. The cult statue of Athena stood within, but rituals happened on the steps outside. Its subtle proportional tricks -- slightly curved columns, inward-leaning walls, a bowed floor -- exist entirely to make the eye perceive perfect geometry where none is actually present.
articles.library: [icon=science 18] The Library is a repository of knowledge -- scrolls, tablets, and codices accumulated from philosophers, explorers, and merchants. Cities with a Library generate more Science each turn, accelerating research into new technologies. City Yields Science +2 Production 90 Requirements Unlocked at Writing. Requires the Campus district. Historical Notes The oldest library yet discovered belongs not to Greece or Rome but to the Assyrian king Ashurbanipal (668-627 BCE), unearthed at Nineveh in the 1840s. His palace library contained over 30,000 clay tablets covering medicine, astronomy, mythology, and administration -- including a copy of the Epic of Gilgamesh that preserved the flood narrative for three millennia until its modern rediscovery. The Great Library of Alexandria was less a single building than an institution: a research complex where Ptolemaic kings funded hundreds of scholars to live, argue, and write. Eratosthenes calculated the circumference of the Earth to within 2% and devised the first system for cataloguing books by genre and author. At its peak the collection may have held 700,000 scrolls. The Islamic House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma), established in Baghdad around 830 CE, preserved and extended classical knowledge at a time when European access to Greek texts had largely vanished. Al-Khwarizmi's algebraic work there gave us both the word 'algorithm' (a Latinisation of his name) and the foundations of the mathematical notation used in every computer on Earth today.
articles.barracks: [icon=warrior 18] The Barracks is a military training facility built in the heart of a city. Its veteran drill instructors turn raw recruits into disciplined soldiers, giving every unit trained here a head start in combat experience. The most fundamental graduate of the Barracks is the Warriors -- the backbone of every early army. With a Barracks in place, Warriors emerge already hardened by months of drilling, formation work, and mock combat, making them meaningfully more durable than unblooded levies. Effects +15 XP for every unit trained in this city. +1 Great General point per turn. Production Cost 80 shields Maintenance 1 gold / turn Historical Notes The word barracks derives from the Spanish barraca -- a rough hut. Early armies billeted soldiers wherever shelter existed: commandeered inns, church halls, stables. Dedicated military barracks only became widespread in the 18th century, driven by the need to house the large standing armies of absolute monarchies without letting them dissolve into civilian life between campaigns. Frederick the Great of Prussia built barracks across Brandenburg and Silesia specifically to prevent desertions -- a soldier in a barracks is under constant supervision; a soldier quartered with a townsman has a hundred opportunities to slip away. The same logic applies in every age: the barracks is as much a mechanism of control as a place of training. Before the modern barracks era, training happened in the open. Roman legionaries swung weighted swords at wooden posts in the campus martius. Greek epheboi drilled in the gymnasium under the eye of retired veterans. In both cases the goal was the same: to replace instinct with habit so that the trained soldier acts correctly even when terrified.
articles.pottery: [icon=science 18] Pottery is the quiet revolution that made settled life possible -- fired clay vessels to store grain, water, oil, and seed against the lean months. Unlocks - Granary -- faster growth and stored food. - Hanging Gardens (Wonder). Research Cost 22 science Era Ancient Historical Notes The oldest fired-clay vessels date to around 18,000 BCE in Jiangxi, China -- made by hunter-gatherers thousands of years before farming. But it was the agricultural village that made pottery indispensable: a surplus is only wealth if it can be stored, counted, and defended. The humble pot underwrites the granary, the marketplace, and the tax collector alike.
articles.writing: [icon=science 18] Writing lets a civilisation remember beyond a single lifetime -- contracts, laws, debts, and discoveries set down in a form that outlives the people who made them. Unlocks - Library -- accelerates research. Research Cost 34 science Era Ancient Eureka Meet another civilisation to research Writing at half cost -- contact spreads the idea of recording speech. Historical Notes Writing was invented independently at least three times: in Mesopotamia (cuneiform, c. 3200 BCE), in China (oracle-bone script, c. 1200 BCE), and in Mesoamerica. The first texts were not poetry or scripture but accounting -- tallies of grain, sheep, and silver. Bureaucracy, not literature, drove the invention of the written word.
articles.bronze_working: [icon=science 18] Bronze Working alloys copper with tin to produce a metal harder and sharper than either -- the material that armed the first professional soldiers and forged the first heavy tools. Unlocks - Smithy -- boosts production. - Barracks -- trains hardened units. Research Cost 40 science Era Ancient Historical Notes Because tin and copper rarely occur together, bronze forced the first long-distance trade networks: Cornish tin reached the eastern Mediterranean by 1300 BCE. When those networks collapsed around 1200 BCE, the Bronze Age civilisations fell with them -- a sober lesson in the fragility of supply chains.
articles.sailing: [icon=science 18] Sailing harnesses the wind to carry ships beyond the sight of land, opening the sea as a highway for trade, war, and exploration. Unlocks - Harbor -- coastal yields and naval support. - Colossus (Wonder). Research Cost 48 science Era Ancient Unlocks a Branch The entire maritime branch stays dark until you found a coastal city. A landlocked empire never opens the sea -- the map itself decides whether this path exists for you. Historical Notes The Egyptians rigged square sails on the Nile by 3100 BCE, but it was the Phoenicians who turned sailing into civilisation-shaping power, founding trading colonies from Tyre to Cadiz and circumnavigating Africa, by Herodotus's account, a full two thousand years before the Portuguese.
articles.masonry: [icon=science 18] Masonry is the craft of cutting and laying stone -- walls that turn a settlement into a fortress and monuments that outlast empires. Unlocks - Walls -- defence and order. - Pyramids (Wonder). Research Cost 54 science Era Ancient Requires Pottery Eureka Work a hills tile to research Masonry at half cost -- quarried stone teaches the mason's art. Historical Notes The Great Pyramid of Giza (c. 2560 BCE) used roughly 2.3 million blocks averaging 2.5 tonnes, fitted so precisely that a knife blade cannot slip between them. Dry-stone walling -- no mortar, just gravity and geometry -- is older still, and some Neolithic examples are standing yet.
articles.currency: [icon=science 18] Currency replaces clumsy barter with standardised money, lubricating trade and letting wealth be saved, taxed, and moved across distances. Unlocks - Market -- more gold each turn. Research Cost 72 science Era Classical Requires Bronze Working Eureka Fill your treasury to research Currency at half cost. Historical Notes The kingdom of Lydia struck the first true coins from electrum around 600 BCE, stamped with a royal lion to guarantee their weight. The idea spread so fast that within a century coinage had reached Greece, Persia, and India -- proof that a trusted unit of account is one of the most contagious ideas in history.
articles.philosophy: [icon=science 18] Philosophy is the disciplined pursuit of wisdom -- ethics, logic, and the examined life -- and the civic institutions that grow up around organised thought. Unlocks - Temple -- happiness and culture. - Oracle (Wonder). Research Cost 90 science Era Classical Requires Writing Historical Notes Around the 6th century BCE, in an era the philosopher Karl Jaspers called the Axial Age, thinkers across Eurasia -- Confucius in China, the Buddha in India, the Pre-Socratics in Greece -- independently turned from myth toward reasoned enquiry into how one ought to live. Civilisation has been arguing about their answers ever since.
articles.mathematics: [icon=science 18] Mathematics gives a civilisation the language of number, proportion, and proof -- the foundation of engineering, astronomy, accounting, and war. Unlocks - Aqueduct -- water, growth, and health. Research Cost 108 science Era Classical Requires Masonry, Currency Eureka Build a set of Walls to research Mathematics at half cost -- fortification demands geometry. Historical Notes Babylonian scribes were solving quadratic equations and using a base-60 system -- the source of our 60-minute hour and 360-degree circle -- a thousand years before Pythagoras. Euclid's Elements (c. 300 BCE) then did something new: it proved its results from axioms, and remained the standard textbook for 2,000 years.
articles.literature: [icon=science 18] Literature elevates writing from record-keeping into art and scholarship -- epics, histories, and the great collections that preserve them. Unlocks - Great Library (Wonder). - Parthenon (Wonder). Research Cost 128 science Era Classical Requires Philosophy Eureka Own two Libraries to research Literature at half cost. Historical Notes The Epic of Gilgamesh, set down on clay around 2100 BCE, is the oldest surviving great work of literature -- already wrestling with mortality, friendship, and the limits of kingship. That a Bronze Age poem still speaks to readers four millennia later is the whole argument for the written word.
articles.construction: [icon=science 18] Construction is large-scale building -- the cranes, scaffolds, arches, and organised labour gangs that raise arenas, ramparts, and roads. Unlocks - Colosseum (Wonder). - Great Wall (Wonder). Research Cost 160 science Era Classical Requires Mathematics Unlocks a Branch Mass construction stays locked until you own a world wonder -- a people learns to build at scale only after it has raised something monumental. Historical Notes The Romans turned construction into an industry. Their invention of opus caementicium -- concrete made with volcanic ash -- let them vault the Pantheon's dome, unmatched in size for 1,300 years and still standing, unreinforced, today.
articles.theology: [icon=science 18] Theology is organised religion -- doctrine, clergy, and great houses of worship that bind a people together and project the power of faith. Unlocks - Cathedral -- happiness and culture. - Grand Temple (Wonder). Research Cost 165 science Era Medieval Requires Philosophy Unlocks a Branch The faith branch opens only after you build a Temple in two cities -- doctrine follows devotion. Historical Notes Medieval cathedrals were the largest enclosed spaces in Europe and often took over a century to build -- the masons who laid the foundations of Chartres or Cologne never saw them finished. They were acts of faith in the most literal sense: investments whose payoff lay beyond a single lifetime.
articles.engineering: [icon=science 18] Engineering applies mathematics to the physical world -- gears, levers, water power, and the institutions that train people to wield them. Unlocks - University -- a powerful science boost. Research Cost 190 science Era Medieval Requires Mathematics Eureka Build an Aqueduct to research Engineering at half cost. Historical Notes Roman engineers built aqueducts that maintained gradients of a few centimetres per kilometre across dozens of miles, delivering fresh water to a million people in the capital. The Pont du Gard still carries its channel 49 metres above the river, laid without mortar.
articles.banking: [icon=science 18] Banking turns idle wealth into working capital -- loans, deposits, and credit that let merchants and states do more than their cash on hand allows. Unlocks - Bank -- compounds a city's gold. Research Cost 215 science Era Medieval Requires Currency, Mathematics Eureka Own three Markets to research Banking at half cost. Historical Notes Renaissance Florence ran on the Medici bank, whose double-entry ledgers and international branches financed popes and kings. Their real innovation was the bill of exchange -- a way to move money across Europe without moving a single coin, sidestepping both bandits and the Church's ban on usury.
articles.astronomy: [icon=science 18] Astronomy maps the heavens -- the motions of stars and planets that guide ships, fix calendars, and overturn a civilisation's place in the cosmos. Unlocks - Observatory -- a strong science building. - Copernicus' Observatory (Wonder). Research Cost 250 science Era Medieval Requires Literature, Sailing Unlocks a Branch The stars stay out of reach until you build a Harbor -- sailors navigating by night drive the study of the sky. (Sailing must already be unlocked, so an inland empire never sees this branch.) Historical Notes Copernicus placed the Sun, not the Earth, at the centre in De revolutionibus (1543), launching a scientific revolution. Yet Polynesian navigators had already crossed thousands of miles of open Pacific for centuries, steering by a memorised star compass and the feel of the swells -- astronomy as lived skill, not theory.
articles.guilds: [icon=science 18] Guilds organise artisans and merchants into self-governing bodies that set standards, train apprentices, and concentrate commercial power. Unlocks - Guild Hall -- production and gold. - Grand Bazaar (Wonder). Research Cost 290 science Era Medieval Requires Banking Eureka Build a Bank to research Guilds at half cost. Historical Notes Medieval guilds were the original gatekeepers of quality: a London goldsmith's work was tested and stamped at Goldsmiths' Hall -- the literal origin of the 'hallmark.' They also functioned as cartels, fiercely restricting who could practise a trade, a tension between standards and monopoly that echoes in professional licensing today.
